EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continues across two locations in Micronesia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wotje remains in  Severe Drought (D2). Near-adequate rainfall on Wotje has prevented any worsening drought conditions the last couple weeks. Wotje and Utirik have reverse osmosis units on site. Rainfall was also well above normal for Wotje last month with adequate rainfall so far this month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry weather continues to persist across a few locations in Micronesia so that drought conditions continue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall continues to be below normal at many locations.  Rainfall in April at Yap was 56% of normal and it has entered Extreme Drought (D3). Vegetation and crops are stressed and crops are inedible on the most impacted islands. Wildfires have been occurring on Yap proper. Ulithi remains in Severe Drought(D2) with rainfall at 41% of normal. Woleai enters Severe Drought (D2) with rainfall at 58% of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One location in Micronesia remains in drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kapingamarangi received 2.26 inches of rain on 11/25 from spotty downpours associated with a trade-wind disturbance. This 2.26 inches on 11/25 is more rainfall in one day than the island received during the month of September, October and most of November combined. This rainfall was the only rain for the entire month. Despite this rainfall it is only 29 % of normal.||Kapingamarangi remains in D4 (Exceptional Drought). A task force was sent down from Pohnpei to Kapingamarangi to assess the conditions and bring supplies. The task force reported taro patches and coconuts were half damaged while banana and breadfruit were fully damaged. The local water supply remains unsafe to drink and saltwater has infiltrated the wells. Residents, especially the elderly, are afflicted with gastrointestinal illness and skin sores. It is estimated that the current food and water supply will last about a month. Moral amongst the residents is still moderate to high.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of severe thunderstorm winds and flooding occurred over the Marianas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning scatterometer data depicted a surface trough just to the east of the Marianas. Upper-level analysis also showed an upper low and trough just to the west of the islands. ||The combination of the two casued an area of scattered to numerous showers and|isolated thunderstorms to develop on the trough east of the islands. The scatterometer data did depict higher winds associated with the trough near Tinian and Saipan. By 449 local time winds gusted to 29 knots (33 mph) at the Saipan airport. Gusty winds in the upper 20 to mid 30 knot range persisted across Saipan until 1000 local time with a gust to 36 knots (41mph) at 955. ||Further south the thunderstorms along the trough collapsed and produced a outflow |boundary. This boundary headed west towards Guam. While winds were also gusty across Guam just before noon, the outflow boundary produced strong wind gusts at a few locations over the island. Andersen AFB saw a wind gust to 44 knots (51mph) at 154 local time and at the National Weather Service Office the highest wind gust was 49 knots (56 mph) a 1202. ||The winds caused some damage to trees on Guam. Power outages did occur in a areas over Guam and was reported in Yigo, Dededo, and Yona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance within a weak monsoon brought heavy to torrential rains to central and southern Guam, resulting in flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An MCS-like feature within the ongoing monsoon brought heavy to torrential rainfall over a short period of time to Guam. Rainfall estimates of 2 inches across the north increased to between 7 and 10 inches across southern Guam; most of it falling within a couple hours. Heavy rainfall and flooding was documented through phone calls, rain gages, social media and broadcast media; and through coordination with the Guam Homeland Security/Office of Civil Defense.||Damage estimates ($) are not available as a damage assessment was not performed (as stated by Guam Homeland Security). This is done when a local or presidential declaration has been made.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continues across two locations in Micronesia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall at Kapingamarangi from the 1st to the 29th of Septmber was 0.86 inches which is 10% of normal. This low rainfall caused Kapingamarangi to go from Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3).||Island community water tanks are being tapped. As of the 30th of  September, only three of the six community water tanks are full. 2 tanks are empty and 1 tank is 1/4 full. Observers estimate that the remaining community water supply will last them about 1.5 months with no additional rainfall. Vegetation is mostly brown with no banana or breadfruit left. Taro crops are still producing as of the 30th of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain showers have increased over the area, but not enough to alleviate the drought over Kapingamarangi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kapingamarangi remains in D4 (Exceptional Drought).|2.05 inches of rain fell in December at Kapingamarangi. Trade-wind distrubances have brushed |the atoll but steady showers remain north of the area and rainfall is still inadequate. |Weather Service Office in Pohnpei reports there is only one community water tank left on the island and it is at 10-15% capacity. Kapingamarangi officials report another water tank is full but contaminated. Private tanks are roughly 20% capacity. Residents report taro patches are still brown and dry despite recent rainfall. Officials  report vegetation has turned a little green with the recent rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of severe thunderstorm winds and flooding occurred over the Marianas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As an outflow boundary generated from collapsing thunderstorms east of Guam passed over the island low-level convergence produced an area of brief heavy showers and thunderstorms, mainly over the southwest section of the island. |Rainfall varied over much of the island with 24 hour amounts at the National Weather |Service Office (Central Guam) totaling to 1.08 inches. Andersen AFB saw 2.23 inches |for the day. A USGS rain gage over the southwest section of the island (Alamagosa |Rain Gage near Santa Rita) recored 3.16 inches between 1145 and 1530. Another USGS Gage (Umatic) saw 2.40 inches between 1215 and 1500. A rain gage owned by a private individual (Casa Davis - ISANTA194) just south of Agat recorded 7.90 inches between 1124 and 1359 Guam time. This would equate to 3.06 inches per hour. The accuracy of the gage is uncertain, but it certainly indicates that rainfall was heavy for a short period of time.   |USGS stream gages saw a rapid rise of a few streams over the southern section of Guam.  A stream gage on the Ugum River above Talofofo Falls, rose from a stream height of 1.24 feet at 1150 to 9.98 feet at 1350 which is a 8.74 foot change. A USGS gage on the Imong River near Agat rose from a height of 9.42 feet at 1150 to 17.57 feet at 1305 which is an 8.15 foot change and a gage on the La Sa Fua River|near Umatac rose from a height of 10.8 feet at 1150 to 17.16 feet at 1305L a 6.36 foot change.||Flooding was observed in Umatic as the river in the village rose quickly. There was a report of a cow being washed out to sea (althogh video of the event seemed to show that the animal may have been a horse). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continues across Kapinamarangi but has decreased across Wotje.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rainfall on Kapingamarangi from the 1st to the 28th of October was 0.87 inches, which is 11% of normal. The persisting dry weather pattern caused Kapingamarangi to go from  Extreme Drought (D3) to D4 (Exceptional Drought).||Residents of Kapingamarangi submitted an urgent request for water on 1 Oct. The Federated States Ship (FSS), Independence arrived several days later with 730 gallons of potable water. Efforts are being made to use the ship's desalinization unit to refill Kapingamarangi's empty water tanks. A second relief trip departed Pohnpei Oct 30 to bring additional water and food supplies. ||As of 27 October, five of the six primary water tanks on Kapingamarangi are empty and vegetation is mostly brown.
